items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.q: What is the difference between aerobic and muscular endurance?
items.correct: Aerobic endurance is the cardiovascular system's ability to supply oxygen; muscular endurance is a muscle's ability to contract repeatedly without fatigue.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same component.
items.random_wrong: Aerobic is for legs, muscular for arms.
items.why: Students treat both as 'being able to keep going'. Aerobic endurance is WHOLE-BODY cardio capacity; muscular endurance is LOCALISED to specific muscles (e.g., sit-up reps).

items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

items.q: According to the Inverted U Hypothesis, performance is best at what level of arousal?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Optimal (moderate). “Very low” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Winning a medal is an example of which type of motivation?
items.why: It's Extrinsic. “Intrinsic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many letters are in the SMART goal-setting acronym?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
